column24: The rest of NT defers to the policy of the NT state government.  Measures are not taken to close workplaces. However, all businesses are required to lodge a COVID-19 Safety Plan that must be complied with and reviewed every six months to ensure any required safety responsibilities are addressed. A COVID Safety Supervisor must also be appointed to facilitate the implementation of the COVID-19 Safety Plan.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129174048/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business
column33: The rest of NT defers to the policy of the NT state government.  Policies are still not taken to cancel public events. However, in major population centres, events with more than 500 people require the submission of a COVID-19 Small Event Checklist and the appointment of a COVID-19 Safety Supervisor and displaying a QR code or alternate method of checking in, while events with more than 1000 people require submission of a COVID-19 Event Safety Plan and formal approval from the Chief Health officer, as well as appointment of a COVID-19 Safety Supervisor and displaying a QR code or alternate method of checking in. Outside of major population centres, events with more than 100 people require the submission of a COVID-19 Small Event Checklist and the appointment of a COVID-19 Safety Supervisor and display a QR code or alternate method of checking in, while events with more than 500 people require submission of a COVID-19 Event Safety Plan and formal approval from the Chief Health officer, as well as appointment of a COVID-19 Safety Supervisor and displaying a QR code or alternate method of checking in.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220120165651/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/business-and-work/business/guidelines-for-events-and-gatherings

column60: The rest of NT defers to the policy of the NT state government.  Several regions within NT were under lockdown during this period.  During a lockdown, residents must stay at home and are only permitted to leave for medical treatment, essential goods and services (once per day), essential work (1 hr per day), exercise, providing care and support to someone who cannot support themselves, and emergencies.  The Wessel Islands Archipelago, encompassing Elcho Island including Galiwin’ku and Wessel Island including Martjanba, remain in lockdown, with the end date set at 30 January 2022.  Gunyangara (Ski Beach), Utopia Homelands and Wurrumiyanga entered lockdown from 22 January 2022 to 29 January 2022.  Alice Springs, Amoonguna, Yuendumu, and Yuelamu remain in lockout, during which vaccinated people may live normally within the lockout area, but unvaccinated people are only permitted to leave their homes for medical treatment, essential goods and services, exercise (1 hr per day), providing care and support to someone who cannot support themselves, and emergencies.  Milikapiti entered a lockdown at 3pm on 24 January 2022, set to finish at 2pm 30 January 2022.  Ampilatwatja entered a 48 hour lockdown at 2pm on 28 January 2022.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220130074104/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/current-restrictions  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220205025033/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/updates/items/2022-01-24-nt-covid-19-update-milikapiti-enters-72-hour-lockdown
column69: The rest of NT defers to the policy of the NT state government.  Vaccinated travellers may freely enter NT, but are required to undertake Rapid Antigen Tests upon Days 1, 3, and 6 of arrival. Unvaccinated returning Territorians and those who have received authority to enter NT are allowed to do so, but have to undertake 14 days mandatory supervised quarantine at their own cost. Unvaccinated visitors are not permitted to enter NT. Children aged 15 years and under are considered to be vaccinated for the purposes of entry into the NT only.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220130210929/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/travel/border-entry
column74: As published on the Department of Home Affairs website, international travelers who are not fully vaccinated are only able to enter Australia if they are exempt or they have been granted an individual exemption. However, fully vaccinated Australian citizens, permanent residents and eligible visa holders can travel to and from Australia without needing to apply for a travel exemption provided they provide a negative PCR test. Quarantine requirements in Australia are determined by State and Territory governments.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220114230614/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/vaccinated-travellers  https://web.archive.org/web/20220116112012/https://covid19.homeaffairs.gov.au/travel-restrictions
column77: According to the Services Australia website (as last updated on 20 January 2022), eligible Australians who suffered financial hardship because they were required to quarantine or self-isolate as a result of having COVID-19, being a close contact of someone with COVID-19, or caring for someone who was required to quarantine or self-isolate were able to receive the Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment or the Crisis Payment for National Health Emergency (COVID-19).     The Pandemic Leave Disaster Payment could be received by individuals who were not receiving any income support payments, were not able to access paid leave entitlements, and had less than $10,000 (AUD) in liquid assets.   Those claiming the payment for periods of mandatory isolation after 18 January 2022 could receive $450 if they had lost between 8 hours (or a full day's work) and 20 hours of work, or $750 if they had lost more than 20 hours of work.   Those claiming the payment for periods of mandatory isolation between 9 December 2021 and 17 January 2022 could receive $750 for each 7-day period of quarantine or isolation.  Those claiming the payment for periods of mandatory isolation before 8 December 2021 could receive $1500 for each 14-day period of quarantine or isolation.     The Crisis Payment for National Health Emergency (COVID-19) could be granted to individuals who were receiving or eligible for an income support payment or the ABSTUDY Living Allowance, and were experiencing severe financial hardship. It was equal to a week's pay at the maximum basic rate of the recipient's income support payment or allowance. Individuals could claim a maximum of 2 payments during a 6-month period, or a maximum of one payment per isolation period.     A 50% reduction in the minimum superannuation drawdown rate which was implemented by the Federal Government in May 2021 continued to be in place.     The directions in place between on the 22 Janurary to 30 Janurary were a continuation of existing directions. column103: Continuation of previous policies  According to the Australian Human Rights Commission website (undated) and the Australian Government Department of Health website (as last updated 6 January 2022 and current as of  22 January 2022), mask mandates were set by State and Territory Governments, and were not formally implemented by the Federal Government. The directions in place between 22 January and 30 January 2022 were a continuation of existing directions. column115: According to the Australian Government Department of Health website (as of 30 January 2022), the elderly were advised to reduce their risk of falling ill from COVID-19 by practicing good personal hygiene and physical distancing, getting vaccinated, as well as arranging for medications, groceries and other essential items to be delivered to them.    The Federal Government also supported initiatives to assist the elderly during the COVID-19 pandemic. The elderly could access home care packages, contact their aged care service providers to gain simplified access to urgent services, and the Commonwealth Home Support Program (CHSP) enabled the elderly to access emergency support to live with relatives during the pandemic. Frameworks for mandatory aged care worker vaccination were also applied across the country. Nonetheless, State and Territory Governments were generally responsible for setting aged care mandates in their own jurisdictions. The directions in place between 22-30 January were a continuation of existing directions.  Archived links:  https://web.archive.org/web/20220130014251/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-older-people-and-carers  https://web.archive.org/web/20220122173147/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/coronavirus-covid-19-information-for-older-australians-faqs  https://web.archive.org/web/20220214222122/https://www.health.gov.au/health-topics/aged-care/advice-on-aged-care-during-covid-19/managing-home-care  https://web.archive.org/web/20220128074317/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/information-for-aged-care-providers-workers-and-residents-about-covid-19-vaccines/mandatory-covid-19-vaccination-in-aged-care

column51: On 5 Jan 2022, Government of South Australia, Department for Infrastructure  and Transport published COVID-19 update - impact on train services.    Passengers are reminded to stay home if they are feeling unwell and to social distance where possible when using public transport.    Passengers are also still required to check-in using QR codes and wear face masks while on-board or waiting at a stop or station.    All public transport passengers, staff and the broader South Australian community are strongly encouraged to get vaccinated and continue to follow SA Health advice. column15: In Western Australia, as of 31st of Janurary 2022 proof of vaccination is required for students of TAFEs and colleges of vocational education to access university buildings or apply for certain courses depending on university policies. column77: In Western Australia, household who have to self isolate due to having COVID-19 or being in close contact with a person with COVID-19 and are unable to work and lost at least 8 hours or a full days of work in a 7 day period of quarantine are eliagble for income support. For each 7 day of self-isolation they are able to recieve $450 if they have lost at least 8 hours/a full days of work and less than 20 hours of work; and $750 if they lost 20 hours or more of work. This is greater than 50% of median salary of $36 per hour since August 2020.     Archieve for who can recieve and how much income support :  https://web.archive.org/web/20220328141245/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/who-can-get-pandemic-leave-disaster-payment?context=53117  https://web.archive.org/web/20220328141547/https://www.servicesaustralia.gov.au/how-much-pandemic-leave-disaster-payment-you-can-get?context=53117    Archieve median salary :  https://web.archive.org/web/20220328142041/https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/labour/earnings-and-working-conditions/employee-earnings/latest-release

column24: Starting 6 Jan to 10 January 2022 the whole of the Northern Territory is in a lockout.  From 13 Jan to 20 Jan lockout was localized to NT areas of Amoonguna, Yuendumu and Yuelamu.  While no restrictions are in place for business closures, businesses and organisations are obligated to ensure employees, customers, visitors and participants are fully vaccinated. Unvaccinated people may access essential services only including supermarkets, hospitals or to get takeaway food.    During a lockout, fully vaccinated residents can live normally within a lockout area. Unvaccinated people aged 16 years and over, including people who’ve received only their first dose of a COVID-19 vaccine, must stay at home during a lockout period and are only permitted to leave for essential reasons.     You can’t travel more than 30km from your home when leaving for 1 of the 3 reasons. If you need to go to the hospital and it’s more than 30km from your home, that’s permitted.    http://web.archive.org/web/20220106095319/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220114131655/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/information-about-face-masks

column131: 12 January     Vaccines mandatory in Victoria for:    - Residential aged care facility workers  - Healthcare workers  - Emergency services workers  - Meat and seafood processing workers  - Quarantine accommodation workers  - Food distribution workers  - Education workers      http://web.archive.org/web/20220209160841/https://www.coronavirus.vic.gov.au/worker-vaccination-requirements    Vaccine Mandates vary by region but generally:  - residential aged care facility workers  - health care facility workers  - workers in school or early childhood education and care facilities   - disability workers  - in-home and community aged care workers.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129113258/https://coronavirus.fairwork.gov.au/coronavirus-and-australian-workplace-laws/covid-19-vaccinations-and-the-workplace/covid-19-vaccinations-legislation-and-public-health-orders      From 19 November 2021 Western Australia   The occupations and workforces covered by this policy have been separated into three categories, subject to different timeframes and circumstances, based on risk.    Group 1: Industries determined to have a high-transmission risk, or are a vulnerability risk or are necessary or critical to the safety of the community.  Group 2: Industries and workforces deemed critical to ongoing delivery of business and the function of the community.  Group 3: Lockdown workers.  https://web.archive.org/web/20220125203311/https://www.shrm.org/ResourcesAndTools/hr-topics/global-hr/Pages/coronavirus-Western-Australia-vaccination-policy.aspx    In December 2021 NSW put in place mandatory vaccination requirements to be extended to all primary care and other private sector health service providers across NSW, with these workers required to have had two doses of a COVID-19 vaccine by 31 January 2022. These changes are consistent with requirements in other states and territories, including Victoria, Queensland, and Western Australia.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220129112503/https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/news/Pages/20211216_01.aspx

column24: From 17 January 2022 Galiwin'ku will be in lockdown for 7 days. There are additional lockouts in place.      The lockdown applies to everyone, whether or not they are vaccinated. Essential workers who are fully vaccinated can continue to go to work.    People can only leave home to:    -receive medical treatment, including COVID testing or vaccination  -obtain essential goods and services, like groceries, power tokens and medications — only one household member once per day  -do 1 hour of outdoor exercise per day, with one other person or people from your household — masks are required while exercising  -provide care and support to a family member or person who cannot support themselves, or in case of an emergency    https://web.archive.org/web/20220121170948/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220114131655/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/information-about-face-masks

column24: Only essential workers who are fully vaccinated can continue to go to work. There are lockdowns in place for Ampilatwatja, Milikapiti, Galiwin’ku, Gunyangara, Wurrumiyanga and all land known as the Utopia Homelands. Currently the longest lockdown is for Elcho Island including Galiwin’ku and the Wessel Islands including Martjanba, until 31 January 2022. However lockdowns and lockouts continue to be extended or put in place.     During a lockdown, residents must stay at home and are only permitted to leave for the following four reasons:    - Medical treatment, including COVID testing and vaccination  - For essential goods and services, like groceries, medications or power tokens. Only one household member should visit the store, once per day  - For one hour of outdoor exercise a day within 5 km from your home with one other person or people from your house  - To provide care and support to a family member or person who cannot support themselves, or in the case of an emergency including escaping harm from domestic/family violence .  - Essential workers who are fully vaccinated can continue to go to work.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102206/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/current-restrictions

column69: Lockdowns and lockouts in the NT areas. Although regulations vary, all interstate travel is restricted to some degree. All travelers to the Northern Territory must complete a border entry form. Entry is prohibited from current hotspots or public exposure sites without a prior approved exemption attached to your border entry form. The government of Western Australia (WA) has enacted a controlled interstate border arrangement. All travelers must register online before traveling to Western Australia. The areas where travelers have spent time prior to arriving in Western Australia determine conditions of their entry and any possible quarantine, vaccination, and/or COVID-19 testing requirements. Currently all areas of AUS are considered an extreme risk. You are not permitted to enter without an approval through G2G Pass (external link). Exemptions are restricted to Commonwealth, State and specialist functions only; If permitted entry, you will be directed to undertake 14 days hotel quarantine at a Government approved quarantine facility at your own expense.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220107081429/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102206/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/current-restrictions
column74: No new policy. Continuing from 15 December AUS reopens the borders to fully vaccinated international students and Temporary Graduate (subclass 485) visa holders. Continuing policy restricts other travelers to Australia to fully vaccinated Australians and permanent residents.     All inbound passengers must also provide proof of vaccination status before arrival through the Australia Travel Declaration, unless they are a child under the age of 12 years and 3 months. It is important to note that previous infection with COVID-19 is not considered a medical contraindication for COVID-19 vaccination.    Passengers providing a medical certificate to provide this proof must ensure that the medical certificate is in English and includes the following information:    -their name (this must match their travel identification documents)  -date of medical consultation and details of their medical practitioner  -details that clearly outline that they have a medical condition that means they cannot receive a COVID-19 vaccination (vaccination is contraindicated).    https://web.archive.org/web/20220116005735/https://www.health.gov.au/health-alerts/covid-19/international-travel/inbound  https://web.archive.org/web/20220106102244/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

column115: No new policies. National policy outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://web.archive.org/web/20220129111557/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129111557/https://www.agedcarequality.gov.au/sites/default/files/media/letter-commissioner-residential-approved-providers-being-prepared-for-covid-19-outbreak-22-december-2021.pdf  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column24: Lockdowns have ended but there are lock-ins in place for Ampilatwatja, Palumpa, Milingimbi, Milikapiti, Elcho and Wessel Islands. The lock-in applies until 6 February 2022.    You can’t leave a lock-in area unless you are an authorised officer or essential worker. You don’t need to stay at home during the lock-in — unless you are a COVID-19 positive case or a close contact of a positive case — but you can’t leave the community without an exemption.    Proof of vaccination is required to enter:  - Bars, pubs and nightclubs licensed to sell and consume liquor on premises  - Clubs licensed to sell and consume liquor on premises  - Casinos and licensed gaming venues  - Restaurants with a liquor licence  - Cinema, theatre, concert, music or dance hall.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102206/https://coronavirus.nt.gov.au/stay-safe/slow-spread/current-restrictions
column33: Lock-ins that are in place restrict travel between regions but universally proof of vaccination is required events of differing maximum quotas. Ex. in the NT Community and recreation centres, play centres and public libraries can operate. Proof of vaccination is required to enter:  - pubs and clubs  - casinos  - restaurants  - cinemas and theatres  - ticketed events for more than 500 people in urban centres or more than 100 people in non-urban centres    https://web.archive.org/web/20220205105653/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt
column42: Lock-ins that are in place restrict travel between regions but universally proof of vaccination is required events of differing maximum quotas. Ex. in the NT Community and recreation centres, play centres and public libraries can operate. Proof of vaccination is required to enter:  - pubs and clubs  - casinos  - restaurants  - cinemas and theatres  - ticketed events for more than 500 people in urban centres or more than 100 people in non-urban centres    Events with less than 500 people don't require a checklist or safety plan, however the physical distancing and hygiene principles should still be considered and implemented.    Events with 500 to 1000 people do require completion of a COVID-19 Small Event Checklist. The organiser of the event must also appoint a COVID-19 Safety Supervisor and display a QR code or alternate method of checking in.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220205105653/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/entertainment-and-amusement-venues/nt  https://web.archive.org/web/20220129102123/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/hotspots-and-case-locations/nt

column15: Continuation of Previous policies in that there is no national policy determining whether schools remain open. Whether schools are open or closed is under the jurisdiction of individual states. The Australian Health Protection Principal Committee (AHPPC) is of the position that schools should open and remain open whenever possible.     According to the National Framework for Managing COVID-19 in Schools and Early Childhood Education and Care as of January 2022 ""the National Cabinet agreed ECEC and schools are essential services and their ability to operate and remain open should be prioritised above other community settings where disease transmission occurs. ""    https://web.archive.org/web/20220103074516/https://www.health.gov.au/news/australian-health-protection-principal-committee-ahppc-statement-on-covid-19-schools-and-early-childhood-education-and-care    https://web.archive.org/web/20220213092848/https://www.pm.gov.au/sites/default/files/media/National%20Framework%20for%20Managing%20COVID-19%20in%20Schools%20and%20Early%20Childhood%20Education%20and%20Care.pdf
column24: Local restrictions vary, and continue to be in place. Example Western Australia:  There are no regional variations of the following policies.  As of 21 February, there is no mention of workplace closing in WA, but the following restrictions (Level 2 measures) are in place:  The 2sqm rule, a maximum of 150 patrons (not including staff) where food and beverages are served, with seated service only.  2sqm rule applies to certain other public venues including Perth Zoo, outdoor amusement parks, swimming pools and libraries.  4sqm rule applies to the Crown Perth Complex casino gaming floor, with seated service only.  50 per cent capacity for specified entertainment venues with fixed seating that faces forward, like cinemas, theatres and concert halls.  50 per cent capacity for major stadiums with fixed seating, such as Optus Stadium, RAC Arena, HBF Stadium (main area) and HBF Park.    Masks are required for people aged 8 years and over (year 3 and over for schools):  •	everywhere indoors, other than in the home  •	when on public transport, in taxis and rideshare vehicles  •	at major stadiums and organised public gatherings  •	at hospitals and residential aged care or disability care facilities    Proof of vaccination requirements remain in place for certain businesses and venues.  This is a continuation of existing policy.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220305054830/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/face-masks/wa  https://web.archive.org/web/20220305054339/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/covid-19-coronavirus/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do
column33: Local restrictions vary, and continue to be in place. Example Tasmania:  All public events must comply with the government Covid-19 Safe Events and Activities Framework (last updated from 1 Nov 2021). Measures in place according to the framework include: physical distancing, respiratory & hand hygiene, frequent cleaning & disinfection, 1 person per 2 square meters density limit for small gatherings (250 people indoor - 500 people outdoor) in unseated venues. Event hosting more than 250-500 people must meet greater requirements such as Event Covid-19 Safety Plan, QR codes check-in, facemask (with 1000+people), approval from the public health authority. 	   Licensed events were only open to the vaccinated.   No regional variation was identified.   https://web.archive.org/web/20220220015146/https://www.coronavirus.tas.gov.au/keeping-yourself-safe/gatherings-density-and-physical-distancing#Events-(indoor-and-outdoor)  https://web.archive.org/web/20220220042459/https://www.business.tas.gov.au/coronavirus_information/event_framework
column42: Local restrictions vary, example Queensland:  As of 13 February, according to QLD government, there is no limit on gatherings in public outdoor spaces. Gatherings at a private residence are limited to a maximum of 100 people.  In addition, there are restrictions on people who are not fully vaccinated in settings such as hospitality venues, entertainment venues, stadium, festivals, government owned galleries, museums and libraries, and vulnerable settings.  Masks are required in all indoor settings. Masks are not mandatory in outdoor settings if 1.5m social distancing can be maintained.  From 7 February, businesses only permitted to allow fully vaccinated attendees must continue to collect contact information and proof of vaccination with the QLD Check-in app. Businesses permitted to allow unvaccinated attendees no longer need to collect contact information via the app.  This is a continuation of existing policy.      https://web.archive.org/web/20220225065348/https://www.qld.gov.au/health/conditions/health-alerts/coronavirus-covid-19/current-status/public-health-directions/movements-gatherings  https://web.archive.org/web/20220225041124/https://www.business.qld.gov.au/running-business/covid-19-restrictions/current  http://archive.today/2022.02.26-035924/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/gatherings-and-work/qld

column69: Restrictions vary, example Northern Territory:  Double vaccinated visitors are welcome to enter the Northern Territory without having to quarantine. column24: As of 3 March in the Western Australia region "Level 2" measures are now in effect. Western Australia Level 2 measures are that:  The 2sqm rule, a maximum of 150 patrons (not including staff) where food and beverages are served, with seated service only, applies to high risk venues including the following:  - Hospitality venues  -Fitness venues  -Entertainment venues (for seated entertainment venues – see below)  -Galleries and museums  -Places of worship  -Hairdressers, beauty services, spas, saunas, tattoo and massage parlours  -Nightclubs     -Convention centres and function centres  -Community, recreation or youth centre facilities  -2sqm rule, up to a limit of 500 patrons, applies to certain other public venues including Perth Zoo, outdoor amusement parks, swimming pools and libraries.  -50 % capacity for specified entertainment venues with fixed seating that faces forward, like cinemas, theatres and concert halls (with seated service only) is permitted as an alternative to the 2sqm rule and 150 patron limit.  50 % capacity for major stadiums with fixed seating, such as Optus Stadium, RAC Arena, HBF Stadium (main area) and HBF Park (with seated service only).    Masks are required for people aged 8 years and over (year 3 and over for schools):  •	everywhere indoors, other than in the home  •	when on public transport, in taxis and rideshare vehicles  •	at major stadiums and organised public gatherings  •	at hospitals and residential aged care or disability care facilities    https://archive.ph/4RVp5

column115: No new policies. National policy outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      Under current restrictions, some people cannot visit a residential aged care facility. This includes people who:    - have returned from overseas in the past 14 days  - have been in contact with someone confirmed to have COVID-19 in the past 14 days  - are unwell, including with a fever or symptoms of acute respiratory illness (such as a cough, sore throat, runny nose, shortness of breath)  - need isolation or quarantine (unless directed by and managed per the direction of the local public health unit).    https://web.archive.org/web/20220322082432/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column69: As of 31 March in the Western Australia region "Level 1" measures are in effect which has eased travel but enforces restrictions against travel to Aboriginal communities. To help keep remote Aboriginal communities safe from COVID-19, there are strict travel restrictions in place. Tourists and travellers are not allowed to enter. Vaccination status does not affect this restriction.     Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  - providing essential services and supplies  - going to work or school there  - entering for family or cultural reasons  - delivering a community program or activity  - entering in an emergency.  These people cannot enter a community if they have COVID-19 or symptoms of COVID-19, or if they are waiting for a test result or have been in contact with someone who has COVID-19.  - Travellers can drive through a remote Aboriginal community only if they do not stop or come into contact with any person in the community.  - Police have the power to enforce the travel restrictions, and fines of up to $50,000 can be issued.    Other AUS regional restrictions vary but are more lenient.     https://archive.ph/8tKNt

column15: No new policy. Schools are teaching in-person with certain precautions. Ex. teachers wearing masks, students wearing masks.  On 13 January 2022, National Cabinet agreed to a national framework to promote a nationally consistent approach to the ongoing delivery of high-quality education for all students during COVID-19. This framework ensure all schools are open and be able to stay open, with as little disruption from COVID-19 as possible.    https://archive.ph/crdxs  https://archive.ph/aW6iv
column24: No new policy. Continuing policy from 31 March is Level 1 (Western Australia region). More widely, proof of vaccination requirements remain in place for certain businesses and venues (ex. hospitality) nationally.     Western Australia Level 1 measures are that:  Density and capacity limits  The 2 square metre (2sqm) rule and 500 patron capacity limit applies to:  -Licensed hospitality venues  -Nightclubs    Convention centres and function centres  The 2sqm rule also applies to:  -Fitness venues  -Entertainment venues  -Galleries and museums  -Places of worship  -Hairdressers, beauty services, spas, saunas, tattoo and massage parlours  -Perth Crown Complex  -Community, recreation or youth centre facilities.    Masks are required for people aged 8 years and over (year 3 and over for schools):  - everywhere indoors, other than in the home  - when on public transport, in taxis and rideshare vehicles  - at hospitals and residential aged care or residential disability care facilities.    https://archive.ph/uBFBe

column42: No new policy. Continuing policy from 31 March is Level 1 (Western Australia region). Home gatherings are limited to a total of 10 people indoors and outdoors, except weddings and funerals. Vaccination status does not affect this limit.    Most other areas have either no limits or the nearest restriction is on ~100 people (Tasmania).    https://archive.ph/uBFBe  https://archive.ph/MPW9W
column51: No new policy. Continued policy is that in NSW COVIDSafe capacity on all modes is still at 75 percent.     https://archive.ph/aUZhy
column60: No lockdowns or lock-ins are currently in place.   https://archive.ph/zueKY
column69: No new policy. Continuing policy from 31 March is Level 1 (Western Australia region). Measures are in effect which enforces restrictions against travel to Aboriginal communities. To help keep remote Aboriginal communities safe from COVID-19, there are strict travel restrictions in place. Tourists and travellers are not allowed to enter. Vaccination status does not affect this restriction.     Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  - providing essential services and supplies  - going to work or school there  - entering for family or cultural reasons  - delivering a community program or activity  - entering in an emergency.  These people cannot enter a community if they have COVID-19 or symptoms of COVID-19, or if they are waiting for a test result or have been in contact with someone who has COVID-19.  - Travellers can drive through a remote Aboriginal community only if they do not stop or come into contact with any person in the community.  - Police have the power to enforce the travel restrictions, and fines of up to $50,000 can be issued.    Other AUS regional restrictions vary but are more lenient.     https://archive.ph/8tKNt

The TGA provisionally approved Pfizer for use in Australia on 25 January 2021 (for 16 years and over), 22 July 2021 (for 12 years and over) and 5 December 2021 (for 5 years and over).  https://web.archive.org/web/20220322082907/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/who-can-get-vaccinated  https://web.archive.org/web/20220322083349/https://www.tga.gov.au/covid-19-vaccine-provisional-registrations
column115: No new policy. Continuing from 31 March in the Western Australia region "Level 1" measures are now in effect. Part of these restrictions include external visitor restrictions for residential aged and disability care facilities:  - 2 visitors permitted per resident patient per day  - Masks must be worn by staff and visitors indoors and outdoors  - Proof of vaccination applies for visitors    National policy still in place outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://archive.ph/uBFBe  https://web.archive.org/web/20220322082432/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column42: Starting 14 April the indoor gathering restrictions in Western Australia have been lifted (previously limited to 10 people). Most locations have no restrictions with the exception of Tasmania which has a limit of 100 people in place since 2021.    Gatherings at residential premises – including shacks – are limited to 100 people (including children and babies) at any one time. This limit includes all residents of the household and the people who ordinarily reside at the house or shack.    The household gathering limit of 100 people applies whether the gathering is indoors or outdoors, and for all types of gatherings, including barbecues and celebrations.    https://archive.ph/ZjA9r  https://archive.ph/Rwegp

column74: Starting 17 April restrictions will be relaxed where travelers no longer must provide a negative pre-departure test to enter Australia.    International travellers into and out of Australia will still be required to provide proof of double vaccination against COVID-19.    The following additional measures have ended:  - restrictions on the entry of cruise vessels into and within Australian territory.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220425173740/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-greg-hunt-mp/media/australias-biosecurity-emergency-pandemic-measures-to-end  https://web.archive.org/web/20220425174009/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/

column24: Starting 29 April Western Australia dropped from Level 1 to "Baseline public health and social measures". However Tasmania still has some capacity limits on businesses:   - Hospitality venues such as restaurants, cafés, food courts, pubs, clubs, bars, RSLs and community clubs can accept up to 250 people per undivided indoor space and up to 500 people per undivided outdoor area at any one time.  - The density limit at these premises is 1 person per 2 square metres.  - Patrons at restaurants, cafés, pubs and clubs must provide contact details and the time of entry of at least one member of their group for possible contact-tracing purposes.    https://archive.ph/W2zd9  https://archive.ph/Xa21M

column69: Starting 29 April Western Australia dropped from Level 1 to "Baseline public health and social measures". However Measures are in effect which enforces restrictions against travel to Aboriginal communities. To help keep remote Aboriginal communities safe from COVID-19, there are strict travel restrictions in place. Tourists and travellers are not allowed to enter. Vaccination status does not affect this restriction.     Under the WA Government’s Remote Aboriginal Communities Directions, the only people who can enter a remote Aboriginal community are residents of those communities and those who are:  - providing essential services and supplies  - going to work or school there  - entering for family or cultural reasons  - delivering a community program or activity  - entering in an emergency.  These people cannot enter a community if they have COVID-19 or symptoms of COVID-19, or if they are waiting for a test result or have been in contact with someone who has COVID-19.  - Travellers can drive through a remote Aboriginal community only if they do not stop or come into contact with any person in the community.  - Police have the power to enforce the travel restrictions, and fines of up to $50,000 can be issued.    Other AUS regional restrictions vary but are more lenient.     https://archive.ph/W2zd9  https://archive.ph/MfG6F
column74: No new policy. Continuing from 17 April restrictions are relaxed where travelers no longer must provide a negative pre-departure test to enter Australia.    International travellers into and out of Australia will still be required to provide proof of double vaccination against COVID-19.    The following additional measures have ended:  - restrictions on the entry of cruise vessels into and within Australian territory.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220425173740/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-greg-hunt-mp/media/australias-biosecurity-emergency-pandemic-measures-to-end  https://web.archive.org/web/20220425174009/https://au.usembassy.gov/covid-19-information/
column103: Starting 29 April Western Australia dropped from Level 1 to "Baseline public health and social measures". However Tasmania still has mask requirements.   Anyone aged 12 years and over must wear a face mask in the following indoor areas once they leave their own or someone else’s home — unless they’re exempt:  - hospitals (excluding patients)  - residential aged care facilities (excluding residents) and in-home and community aged care  - residential disability care facilities (excluding residents) and in-home care, including disability support provider and NDIS-funded services  - correctional facilities (excluding prisoners and detainees)  - health care and medical facilities and services/treatments, including allied health  - primary (teachers, staff and visitors only) and secondary schools (including students)  - early childhood services, including childcare  - public transport, including buses, passenger ferries, taxis and rideshare services  - airports and aircraft  - the Spirit of Tasmania and ports  You don’t need to wear a face mask outdoors. However, you may choose to wear one as an extra safety measure.  https://archive.ph/W2zd9  https://archive.ph/xVoyd
column115: Starting 29 April Western Australia dropped from Level 1 to "Baseline public health and social measures". However part of these restrictions include external visitor restrictions for residential aged and disability care facilities (same as previous threat levels):  - 2 visitors permitted per resident patient per day  - Masks must be worn by staff and visitors indoors and outdoors  - Proof of vaccination applies for visitors    National policy still in place outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://archive.ph/W2zd9  https://web.archive.org/web/20220322082432/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://web.archive.org/web/20220712102242/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/covid-19-coronavirus/covid-19-coronavirus-what-you-can-and-cant-do  https://web.archive.org/web/20220322082432/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column115: From 2 September in Western Australia visitation limits will be eased for residential aged care and disability care facilities - individual facilities can determine the number of visitors allowed per resident each day (previously limited to 2 visitors per resident patient per day). Residential aged care and disability care facilities will continue to require masks to be worn by employees and visitors. Proof of vaccination is required to enter residential aged care facilities (not required to enter residential disability care facilities).    National policy still in place outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://web.archive.org/web/20221115112839/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/announcements/mask-rules-and-other-public-health-measures-ease  https://web.archive.org/web/20220905201351/https://www.health.gov.au/node/18602/coronavirus-covid-19-advice-for-people-in-residential-aged-care-facilities-and-visitors  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column74: From 9 September 2022, masks are no longer required on flights travelling to Australia.  https://web.archive.org/web/20221115110643/https://www.health.gov.au/ministers/the-hon-mark-butler-mp/media/removal-of-mask-mandates-on-international-flights

column90: No new policy. Continued from 9 Aug 2022 Australia’s CovidSafe app was decommissioned because it is no longer being used for Covid-19 contact tracing. https://web.archive.org/web/20221128231515/https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2022/aug/10/australia-retires-covidsafe-contact-tracing-app-that-was-barely-used

column15: No new policy. Schools are teaching in-person with certain precautions. Ex. teachers wearing masks, students wearing masks.  On 13 January 2022, National Cabinet agreed to a national framework to promote a nationally consistent approach to the ongoing delivery of high-quality education for all students during COVID-19. This framework ensure all schools are open and be able to stay open, with as little disruption from COVID-19 as possible.    - School staff and regular visitors to schools must be fully vaccinated. They must receive a booster dose within a 1 month of becoming eligible.    - Students are encouraged to be vaccinated as soon as possible.    Technical note: at the start of October Coronavirus (COVID-19) information for schools and student moved to a new website because the Department of Education, Skills and Employment became the Department of Education and a new entity was created, the Department of Employment and Workplace Relations. The Department of Education, Skills and Employment website was decommissioned.    https://web.archive.org/web/20220925082829/https://www.education.gov.au/covid-19/schools  https://web.archive.org/web/20221018092035/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/education-and-childcare/wa
column24: No policy change. There are no longer capacity or density limits. There are no vaccination rules for entry into venues. The TGA provisionally approved Pfizer for use in Australia on 25 January 2021 (for 16 years and over), 22 July 2021 (for 12 years and over) and 5 December 2021 (for 5 years and over).  https://web.archive.org/web/20221101211719/https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/who-can-get-vaccinated  https://web.archive.org/web/20221028124538/https://www.tga.gov.au/products/covid-19/covid-19-vaccines/covid-19-vaccine-provisional-registrations
column115: No new policy. Continuing from 2 September in Western Australia, there are no longer visitation quantity limitations, but residential aged care facilities require proof of vaccination to enter and masks to be worn by employees and visitors.     National policy still in place outlines recommended guidelines to protect the elderly along a three tiered system. The visitation restrictions and guidelines for residential aged care facilities follow social distancing, vaccination and COVID screening norms. It is up to providers to manage and mitigate COVID-19 risks to aged care residents, including the risks that isolation poses to their physical, social and emotional wellbeing. Face-to-face get togethers between residents, family members and friends should be facilitated where they can be undertaken safely and are permitted under public health orders. Residents of an aged care facility have the right to choose to have vaccinations or not.      https://web.archive.org/web/20221101014109/https://www.wa.gov.au/government/covid-19-coronavirus/covid-19-coronavirus-proof-of-covid-19-vaccinations  https://web.archive.org/web/20210415104420/https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/2020/11/coronavirus-covid-19-national-aged-care-guidance-escalation-tiers-and-aged-care-provider-responses.pdf

column15: No new policy. Schools are teaching in-person with certain precautions. Ex. teachers wearing masks, students wearing masks.  On 13 January 2022, National Cabinet agreed to a national framework to promote a nationally consistent approach to the ongoing delivery of high-quality education for all students during COVID-19. This framework ensure all schools are open and be able to stay open, with as little disruption from COVID-19 as possible.    - School staff and regular visitors to schools must be fully vaccinated. They must receive a booster dose within a 1 month of becoming eligible.    - Students are encouraged to be vaccinated as soon as possible.    https://web.archive.org/web/20221115085916/https://www.education.gov.au/covid-19/schools  https://web.archive.org/web/20221115085938/https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/covid19-restriction-checker/education-and-childcare/wa
